title: Combined & Expanded Uncertainty: Propagation, Welch–Satterthwaite, Coverage
summary: Once every input has a standard uncertainty $u(x_j)$, this lesson combines them. The **law of propagation of variance** $u^2(y)=\sum_j (\partial G/\partial x_j)^2 u^2(x_j)=\sum_j c_j^2 u^2(x_j)$ turns input variances into the combined variance through **sensitivity coefficients** $c_j$ (uncorrelated inputs drop the covariance terms). The **Welch–Satterthwaite** formula gives the **effective degrees of freedom** $\nu_y=\lfloor u^4(y)/\sum_j (u_j^4(y)/\nu_j)\rfloor$. Finally the **expanded uncertainty** $U=k\,u(y)$ scales by a coverage factor $k$ read from the Student-$t$ at the chosen confidence (95% → ~2), and the result is reported as $y\pm U$.
objectives: Write a measurand as a model $y=G(x_1,\dots,x_q)$ and linearise it with a first-order Taylor expansion.
Compute a sensitivity coefficient $c_j=\partial G/\partial x_j$ analytically or as a numerical incremental ratio.
Apply the law of propagation of variance $u^2(y)=\sum_j c_j^2 u^2(x_j)$ for uncorrelated inputs, and know that only $c_j^2$ enters (sign irrelevant).
Recognise the variance–covariance matrix form $u^2(y)=\{c\}^T[s^2(x)]\{c\}$ and when covariances may be set to zero.
Handle input/output multiplicities $n_d,n_r$ when an input or the result refers to an average ($s^2(m)=s^2(x)/n$).
Compute the effective degrees of freedom by the Welch–Satterthwaite formula and floor the result.
Choose the coverage factor $k=t_{1-\alpha/2,\nu_y}$ from the Student-$t$ for a stated confidence (95% → ~2, 98%, 99%), compute $U=k\,u(y)$, and report $y\pm U$.

blocks.text: 1. The measurement model and its linearisation

A measurand $Y$ is almost never read directly: it is a **function** of several input quantities $X_j$,
$$Y=G(X_1,\dots,X_j,\dots,X_q),$$
each known only with some uncertainty. The mean of the output follows the mean of the inputs, $\bar y=G(\bar x_1,\dots,\bar x_q)$. For a small variation $\delta x_j$ of each input, a **first-order Taylor expansion** (valid when $G$ is regular near the operating point) gives the output variation:

blocks.tex: \delta y=\left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial x_1}\right)\delta x_1+\dots+\left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial x_q}\right)\delta x_q
blocks.caption: Linearised relation between an output deviation and the input deviations, evaluated at the mean values. This is the basis of the whole propagation law.

blocks.text: The partial derivative $c_j=\left(\dfrac{\partial G}{\partial x_j}\right)_{\bar x_j}$, evaluated at the nominal inputs. It quantifies **how strongly $Y$ responds** to a variation of $X_j$ and carries the **units** that convert $u(x_j)$ into the units of $u(y)$. For a model given as a formula, compute it analytically (e.g. $y=kx\Rightarrow c_k=x,\ c_x=k$; $y=\prod x_i\Rightarrow c_j=y/x_j$); for a model given as a procedure or empirical diagram, approximate it numerically.

blocks.tex: c_j\approx\frac{G(\bar x_1,\dots,\bar x_j+\Delta x_j,\dots,\bar x_q)-G(\bar x_1,\dots,\bar x_j,\dots,\bar x_q)}{\Delta x_j}
blocks.caption: Numerical sensitivity coefficient (incremental ratio). The perturbation $\Delta x_j$ must be small enough not to be affected by local nonlinearity.

blocks.text: 2. The law of propagation of variance

Replicating the linearised relation over $n$ measurements and forming the variance of $Y$ leads, in matrix form, to $s^2(y)=\{c_j\}^T[s^2(x)]\{c_j\}$, where $[s^2(x)]$ is the **variance–covariance matrix** of the inputs: variances on the diagonal, covariances off it. When the influence quantities are **strongly random** and there is **no specific indication of dependence**, the covariances are taken **null** and the matrix becomes diagonal. The result is the central formula of the course:

blocks.tex: u^2(y)=\sum_{j=1}^{q}\left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial x_j}\right)^2 u^2(x_j)=\sum_{j=1}^{q} c_j^2\,u^2(x_j)=\sum_{j=1}^{q} u_j^2(y)
blocks.caption: Law of propagation of variance (uncorrelated inputs). Each contribution $u_j^2(y)=c_j^2\,u^2(x_j)$ is a sensitivity-squared times an input variance; the combined variance is their **sum**, and $u(y)=\sqrt{u^2(y)}$ — take the root **once**, at the end.

blocks.title: Variances add, then root once
blocks.text: Build a budget **row by row**: for each input compute $u^2(x_j)$, the sensitivity $c_j$, and the product $u_j^2(y)=c_j^2 u^2(x_j)$. Sum the products to get $u^2(y)$, and only then take $u(y)=\sqrt{u^2(y)}$. Squaring early or rooting each row is a frequent error. The same equation can be written with both Type A and Type B inputs, because each is just a standard uncertainty $u$.

blocks.title: Why the sensitivity is squared (sign drops out)
blocks.text: Only $c_j^2$ enters, so a **negative** sensitivity (e.g. $c=-1.60$ or $c=-4000$ in the canonical budget) contributes exactly as much as a positive one of the same magnitude. The sign matters only when discussing *correlation* or *which way* an input pushes the result — never in the budget sum, where each $c_j^2 u^2(x_j)\ge 0$.

blocks.title: Differences inflate uncertainty
blocks.text: For $Y=X_2-X_1$ the sensitivities are $\pm1$, so $u^2(y)=u^2(x_1)+u^2(x_2)$ — **larger** than either input's variance. In *relative* terms this is worse the closer $X_1$ and $X_2$ are, because the difference shrinks while its uncertainty does not. (No issue when $X_1$ is just a small correction to $X_2$.)
3. Multiplicities: when an input or the result is an average

By the Central Limit Theorem the **variance of an average** of $n$ data is the single-value variance divided by $n$: $s^2(m)=s^2(x)/n$. Whether you must multiply or divide depends on what the information refers to:

- **Input multiplicity $n_d$**: the variability information was obtained on a mean of $n_d$ data → convert to a single-value variance with $s^2(x)=n_d\,s^2(m)$.
- **Output multiplicity $n_r$**: the final result is the average of $n_r$ measurements → $s^2(m)=s^2(x)/n_r$.

In one column this is the factor $u^2(x_j)=\dfrac{a_j^2}{k_{a}}\cdot\dfrac{n_d}{n_r}$ (Type B) or $s_j^2\cdot\dfrac{n_d}{n_r}$ (Type A).

blocks.title: Average vs individual value — do not mix them
blocks.text: Variability that refers to a **mean** ($s^2(m)$) and variability that refers to an **individual value** ($s^2(x)$) differ by a factor $n$. Using the wrong one (e.g. quoting the spread of single parts when designing for the *mean* tensile strength, or vice versa) leads to serious mistakes. Always read whether the quoted figure is per-measurement or per-average.
4. The uncertainty budget table

Organising the propagation law as a **table** (the *uncertainty budget*) gives a global view of the **weight** of each contribution. One row per input lists its value/note, the Type A $s_j$ or the Type B half-width $a_j$ and divisor $k_a$, the resulting $u^2(x_j)$, the sensitivity $c_j$, and the contribution $u_j^2(y)=c_j^2 u^2(x_j)$; the footer sums to $u^2(y)$ and roots to $u(y)$.

blocks.title: Two table rules worth marks
blocks.text:
- **Same multiple/submultiple for every unit (base units).** Keep the whole table homogeneous (µm→m, etc.) so the sensitivity coefficients automatically carry the right units and you need not relabel.
- **Absolute vs relative.** Do not mix contributions given as an absolute value (with a unit) and as a relative value (often a percentage); non-dimensional quantities need special care.
The biggest $u_j^2(y)$ tell you where to spend money to reduce uncertainty; the smallest tell you where you may relax the instrument to cut cost.

blocks.title: Canonical budget — a length corrected to 20 °C

blocks.text: 5. Effective degrees of freedom: Welch–Satterthwaite

The combined $u(y)$ mixes contributions with different reliabilities: a Type A average has $
u=n-1$, a regression slope $
u=n-n_p$, a Type B value a conventional 100 / 30 / 15. To pick one coverage factor we need a single **effective** number of degrees of freedom for $y$. GUM uses the **Welch–Satterthwaite** formula, which relates each contribution's variance $u_j^2(y)$ and its d.o.f. $
u_j$ to the combined variance and its d.o.f. $
u_y$:

blocks.caption: Welch–Satterthwaite. The numerator is the combined variance squared; each denominator term is a contribution's variance squared over its own d.o.f. Take the **floor** (Excel INT).

blocks.title: Floor it — never round up
blocks.text:
Always take the **floor** ($=$INT$(\cdot)$). In the canonical budget the raw value is $
u_y\approx15.7$, which becomes $
u_y=15$ — not 16. Rounding up would inflate the d.o.f., shrink $k$, and **understate** $U$; flooring keeps the interval conservative. (With many strong Type B contributions $
u_y$ can instead come out large, e.g. 64, so $k	o 2$.)
6. Expanded uncertainty and coverage factor

The combined $u(y)$ is a **standard deviation**: for a normal output the band $\pm u$ leaves out more than 30% of the values, so it does not give a *reasonable* coverage. The GUM therefore scales it to a confidence interval. The procedure: **(1)** fix the risk $\alpha=1-P$ (in metrology, $P=95\%$, symmetric two-sided, is conventional when consequences are unknown); **(2)** determine the interval boundaries from the Student-$t$ once $\nu_y$ is known (using $t$ rather than the normal $z$ avoids underestimating the uncertainty); **(3)** apply the coverage factor.

For finite degrees of freedom $k$ is a **Student-$t$ quantile**, not the asymptotic normal $1.96$: $k=t_{1-\alpha/2,\,
u_y}=$ T.INV$\left(1-	frac{1-P}{2},\ 
u_yight)$. As $
u_y	o\infty$ the $t$-quantile approaches $z$ (e.g. $1.96$ at 95%). In the metrological field the convention is simply $k=2$ ($\approx$ the $2\sigma$ level, $\approx95\%$ with many d.o.f.).

blocks.tex: U(y)=k\cdot u(y),\qquad\text{report}\quad y=\bar y\pm U(y)\ \text{at confidence } P
blocks.caption: Expanded uncertainty: the half-width of the confidence interval reported with the result. The expression of uncertainty is complete only if $P$ (and the d.o.f. used) are also stated. If a value is quoted simply as “uncertainty”, it is to be read as the expanded uncertainty.

blocks.title: The Student-t behind the coverage factor k
blocks.caption:
Lower the degrees of freedom and watch the tails fatten: the two-sided 95% quantile $t_{0.975,
u}$ climbs above the normal $1.96$ (e.g. $2.13$ at $
u=15$, $3.18$ at $
u=3$). That extra width is exactly why we floor $
u_y$ and use $t$, not $z$, for finite samples.

blocks.title: Coverage factors at a glance
blocks.text:
Two-sided coverage factors for large $
u$ ($	o$ normal $z$): **90% → 1.645**, **95% → 1.96** (rounded to 2 by convention), **98% → 2.33**, **99% → 2.58**. For a finite $
u_y$ each is larger, read from T.INV$(1-\alpha/2,
u_y)$: at $
u_y=15$ and 95%, $k=2.131$.

blocks.title: Finishing the canonical budget
blocks.text:
From $u(y)=3.607\ \mu$m and the six contributions, Welch–Satterthwaite gives $\nu_y=\lfloor15.7\rfloor=15$. At $P=95\%$ the coverage factor is $k=$T.INV$(0.975,15)=2.131$ — a Student-$t$ value, larger than $1.96$ because $\nu_y$ is finite. Hence
$$U=k\,u(y)=2.131\times3.607\ \mu\text{m}=7.69\ \mu\text{m}.$$
The result is reported as $L_{20}=800.0748\ \text{mm}\pm7.7\ \mu$m at 95% confidence.

blocks.title: Full budget checklist
blocks.text: 1. Convert every datum to **base units**. 2. For each input: Type A or B? distribution? right divisor? include any $n_d/n_r$ factor → $u^2(x_j)$. 3. Sensitivity $c_j$, then $u_j^2(y)=c_j^2 u^2(x_j)$. 4. $u^2(y)=\sum u_j^2(y)$, then $u(y)=\sqrt{\,\cdot\,}$. 5. $\nu_y$ by Welch–Satterthwaite, **floored**. 6. $k=$T.INV$(1-\alpha/2,\nu_y)$. 7. $U=k\,u(y)$; report $y\pm U$ at $P$.

question.prompt: In the law of propagation $u^2(y)=\sum_j c_j^2 u^2(x_j)$, one input has a **negative** sensitivity $c_j=-4000$. How does it enter the combined variance?

options.content: It subtracts from the variance because $c_j<0$
It is dropped, since variances must be positive
Its sign flips the sign of the final $u(y)$

question.explanation: Only $c_j^2$ enters, so the sign disappears: $(-4000)^2=(+4000)^2$ and the contribution is positive. **A** misreads the formula — for uncorrelated inputs there is no subtraction. **B** would discard a real contribution. **D** is meaningless: $u(y)=\sqrt{u^2(y)}\ge0$ always. The sign of $c_j$ only matters for correlation or for the direction of the effect.
question.theory: Uncorrelated propagation: every contribution $c_j^2 u^2(x_j)\ge0$; the sign of $c_j$ is irrelevant to the sum.

practice.prompt: What does a **sensitivity coefficient** $c_j$ represent in an uncertainty budget?

options.content: The partial derivative $\partial G/\partial x_j$ at the nominal inputs
The number of degrees of freedom of input $x_j$

practice.explanation: $c_j=(\partial G/\partial x_j)_{\bar x_j}$ is the partial derivative of the model evaluated at the mean inputs; it scales how an input's uncertainty propagates to $y$ and carries the unit conversion. **B** is $u(x_j)$, a separate column. **C** is $\nu_j$, used later in Welch–Satterthwaite. **D** confuses a sensitivity with a covariance, which lives off-diagonal in the variance–covariance matrix.
practice.theory: $c_j=\partial G/\partial x_j$; the contribution is $u_j^2(y)=c_j^2 u^2(x_j)$.

practice.prompt: Inputs to a budget are uncorrelated, with $u_1^2(y)=4.0$, $u_2^2(y)=9.0$, $u_3^2(y)=0.33$ (units of $\mu\text{m}^2$). The combined standard uncertainty $u(y)$ is closest to:
practice.explanation: Variances add: $u^2(y)=4.0+9.0+0.33=13.33\ \mu\text{m}^2$, then root once: $u(y)=\sqrt{13.33}=3.65\ \mu$m. **A** forgets the square root and reports the variance. **C** roots only the largest term ($\sqrt9$+... incorrectly). **D** wrongly takes the square root of each term first and... no — it averages; in any case it ignores that variances add then root once.
practice.theory: $u^2(y)=\sum u_j^2(y)$; $u(y)=\sqrt{u^2(y)}$ — sum the variances, take the root at the end.

practice.prompt: A quantity is computed as the **difference** $Y=X_2-X_1$, with $u(x_1)=3$ and $u(x_2)=4$ (same units), uncorrelated. The standard uncertainty $u(y)$ is:
practice.explanation: Sensitivities are $c_1=-1$, $c_2=+1$, so $u^2(y)=(-1)^2 3^2+(1)^2 4^2=9+16=25$, $u(y)=5$. **B** adds the uncertainties linearly instead of in quadrature. **C** subtracts them — uncertainties never subtract. **D** is unrelated. Note $u(y)=5$ exceeds *both* input uncertainties: differences inflate uncertainty.
practice.theory: For $Y=X_2-X_1$: $u^2(y)=u^2(x_1)+u^2(x_2)$ — uncertainties combine in quadrature, never by subtraction.

practice.prompt: Welch–Satterthwaite gives a raw effective degrees of freedom $\nu_y=15.7$. What value goes into the coverage factor, and why?

options.content: $15$ — take the floor (INT), to stay conservative

practice.explanation: The convention (and the sheet's INT) is to **floor** to $15$. Fewer d.o.f. → fatter-tailed $t$ → larger $k$ → larger, safely conservative $U$. **A** rounds up and would shrink $k$. **B** feeds a non-integer where an integer d.o.f. is expected. **C** is false — a handful of inputs does not give infinite d.o.f.; that limit is only asymptotic.
practice.theory: Always floor the Welch–Satterthwaite $\nu_y$ (INT) before reading $k$ from T.INV.

practice.prompt: With a combined $u(y)=3.607\ \mu$m and effective degrees of freedom $\nu_y=15$, the expanded uncertainty at 95% confidence ($k=t_{0.975,15}=2.131$) is closest to:
practice.explanation: $U=k\,u(y)=2.131\times3.607=7.69\ \mu$m. **A** uses the asymptotic normal $1.96$, valid only for $\nu\to\infty$; with $\nu_y=15$ the Student-$t$ value $2.131$ is required. **C** reports $u(y)$ itself, forgetting that the expanded uncertainty multiplies by $k$. **D** divides by $k$ — that is the inverse operation (used to go from expanded back to standard).
practice.theory: $U=k\,u(y)$ with $k=t_{1-\alpha/2,\nu_y}$; report $y\pm U$ at the stated confidence.

practice.prompt: Why does the GUM use the **Student-$t$** quantile for the coverage factor instead of the normal value $1.96$ at 95%?

options.content: Because the inputs are never normal
Because with finite degrees of freedom the t-distribution has fatter tails, so $z=1.96$ would underestimate the uncertainty
Because $t$ is easier to compute than $z$
Because the confidence level changes from 95% to 99%

practice.explanation: For a finite $\nu_y$ the combined uncertainty is itself uncertain, giving the $t$-distribution heavier tails than the normal; using $z=1.96$ would yield too narrow an interval and **underestimate** $U$. As $\nu_y\to\infty$, $t\to z$. **A** is irrelevant — by the CLT the output is taken normal; it is the *estimated* standard deviation that forces $t$. **C** is false. **D** does not happen — the confidence is fixed at the chosen $P$.
practice.theory: Coverage factor $k=t_{1-\alpha/2,\nu_y}\ge z$; equality only as $\nu_y\to\infty$. Using $t$ avoids underestimating $U$.

practice.prompt: In the **metrological field**, when the consequences of error are unknown, what confidence level and coverage factor are conventionally adopted?
practice.explanation: Conventionally metrology reports at the **$2\sigma$ level**, i.e. $P=95\%$ with a symmetric two-sided risk and $k\approx2$ (exactly 2 for many d.o.f.). **A** is the bare $\pm1\sigma$ band, which leaves out >30% of values — not a reasonable coverage. **B** is the $3\sigma$ level, not the convention. **D** is far too narrow to be useful.

practice.prompt: A reproducibility input is quoted as a **variance** $V=10.7\ \mu\text{m}^2$ (per single measurement), and the reported result is the **average of $n_r=5$** measurements. What variance $u^2(x_j)$ enters the budget for this input?
practice.explanation: The variance of an average of $n_r$ values is the single-value variance divided by $n_r$: $V/n_r=10.7/5=2.14\ \mu\text{m}^2$. **A** ignores the output multiplicity (averaging). **B** confuses a standard deviation with a variance — the $u^2(x_j)$ column holds variances. **C** multiplies, which would be the variance of a *sum*, not a mean.
practice.theory: Output multiplicity: variance of the mean $=$ single-value variance $/n_r$. Keep variances (not std-devs) in the $u^2(x_j)$ column.

practice.prompt: Under what condition may the **covariance** terms be dropped from the propagation law, leaving $u^2(y)=\sum_j c_j^2 u^2(x_j)$?

options.content: When all sensitivity coefficients are positive
When the inputs are uncorrelated (no specific indication of dependence)
When all inputs are Type B
When the degrees of freedom are infinite

practice.explanation: The full form $u^2(y)=\{c\}^T[s^2(x)]\{c\}$ keeps off-diagonal covariances; these vanish when the influence quantities are treated as **uncorrelated** (no indication of dependence), making the matrix diagonal. **A** is irrelevant — the sign of $c_j$ does not control correlation. **C** is false: Type A or B has nothing to do with correlation between inputs. **D** confuses degrees of freedom with covariance.
practice.theory: Uncorrelated inputs → covariances null → diagonal variance–covariance matrix → $u^2(y)=\sum c_j^2 u^2(x_j)$.

practice.prompt: How should a complete measurement result be **reported** at the end of a GUM budget?

options.content: As $y$ alone, since the uncertainty is internal
As $y\pm u(y)$, the combined standard uncertainty
As $y\pm U(y)$ with the confidence level $P$ (and the d.o.f. used) stated

practice.explanation: A result is reported as $y\pm U(y)$ at a stated confidence $P$, where $U=k\,u(y)$; the expression is complete only if $P$ (and the degrees of freedom used) accompany it. **A** omits the uncertainty entirely. **B** reports only the standard uncertainty ($\pm1\sigma$), which leaves out >30% of the values. **D** reports a variance (wrong units, and unscaled).
practice.theory: Report $y\pm U$ at confidence $P$, with $U=k\,u(y)$; state $P$ and the d.o.f. for a complete expression.
